title: Javascript shortcut
content:
✅ Summary (Part 7)

  • Real website pe DOM practice ki

  • Multiple elements select kiye

  • forEach se manipulate kiya

  • Styling + content change kiya

  • Correct selector choose karna seekha

🎯 FINAL UNDERSTANDING:

  • Element select karo

  • Loop lagao (agar multiple ho)

  • Style / content change karo

👉 यही है DOM manipulation ka core 💯

🔥 Agar chaho to next mai main tumhe:

  • Pure revision cheat sheet

  • ya practice questions + mini project

bhi de sakta hoon 👍

continue

📒 Part 8 Notes (Final Wrap + Real Understanding + Mindset) – Hinglish

🔹 Ab Tak Kya Seekha (Big Picture)

👉 DOM manipulation ka pura flow:

  1. Element select karo

  2. Usko variable me store karo

  3. Uski properties access karo

  4. Style / content change karo

🔹 Core Methods Recap

  • getElementById

  • querySelector

  • querySelectorAll

  • getElementsByClassName

👉 Inhi se 90% kaam ho jata hai

🔹 Core Properties Recap

  • .style → CSS change

  • .innerText → visible text

  • .textContent → full text

  • .innerHTML → HTML ke saath

🔹 Important Thinking Pattern 🧠

👉 Har problem me socho:

  • Kya mujhe single element chahiye?
    → querySelector

  • Kya mujhe multiple elements chahiye?
    → querySelectorAll

🔹 Loop Use Karna

👉 Jab multiple elements ho:

elements.forEach(el => {
    el.style.color = "blue"
})

🔹 Real World Use Case

👉 Suppose:

  • Database se 10 items aaye

  • Already 10 <li> hain page pe

👉 Tum kya karoge?

  • querySelectorAll("li")

  • loop lagao

  • data fill karo

🔹 Important Concept (Very Important ⚠️)

👉 JavaScript console me jo kiya:

  • Same cheez:

    • <script> tag me likh sakte ho

    • ya .js file me

🔹 Mistakes Jo Sab Karte Hain ❌

  1. Wrong selector use karna

  2. HTMLCollection pe direct loop lagana

  3. innerHTML misuse karna

  4. Case sensitivity ignore karna

🔹 Best Practices ✅

  • Prefer querySelector & querySelectorAll

  • Clean selectors use karo (class/id)

  • Loop properly use karo

  • Debug using console

🔹 Powerful Realization 💡

👉 Frameworks (React, Angular, etc.):

  • Internally yehi sab karte hain

👉 Isliye:

  • JS DOM strong = future safe

🔹 Final लाइन (Golden Line ⭐)

👉
"Agar tum element select karna samajh gaye, to tum DOM control kar sakte ho."

🧠 1. DOM kya hai?

  • DOM = Document Object Model

  • HTML ko tree structure me represent karta hai
    👉 Parent → Child → Sibling

🧠 2. Element select karna

document.querySelector('.class')
document.getElementById('id')

🧠 3. Parent & Children

✔️ Parent select:

const parent = document.querySelector('.parent')

✔️ Children:

parent.children   // HTMLCollection

✔️ Length:

parent.children.length

✔️ Access:

parent.children[0]

🧠 4. Loop through children

for (let i = 0; i < parent.children.length; i++) {
  console.log(parent.children[i].innerHTML)
}

🧠 5. Important Properties

✔️ First child:

parent.firstElementChild

✔️ Last child:

parent.lastElementChild

🧠 6. Child → Parent

child.parentElement

✔️ Efficient (avoid re-querying DOM)

🧠 7. Sibling access

child.nextElementSibling
child.previousElementSibling

🧠 8. children vs childNodes 🔥

PropertyOutput
.childrenOnly elements
.childNodesText + comment + elements

⚠️ Important

parent.childNodes

👉 Includes:

  • line breaks (text nodes)

  • comments

  • elements

🧠 9. NodeList vs HTMLCollection

FeatureHTMLCollectionNodeList
Source.children.childNodes
ContainsOnly elementsAll nodes

🧠 10. Create Element

const div = document.createElement('div')

✔️ Memory me banta hai
❌ Page pe visible nahi hota

🧠 11. Add attributes

✔️ Class:

div.className = "main"

✔️ ID:

div.id = "myId"

✔️ Custom:

div.setAttribute("title", "value")

🧠 12. Styling

div.style.backgroundColor = "green"
div.style.padding = "10px"

🧠 13. Text add karna

❌ Less optimal:

div.innerText = "Hello"

✅ Better:

const text = document.createTextNode("Hello")
div.appendChild(text)

🧠 14. Attach to DOM (MOST IMPORTANT 🔥)

document.body.appendChild(div)

✔️ Tabhi element page pe dikhega

🧠 15. Full Flow (Remember this)

const div = document.createElement('div')

div.className = "main"
div.id = "myId"

div.setAttribute("title", "test")

const text = document.createTextNode("Hello")
div.appendChild(text)

document.body.appendChild(div)

🎯 Final Key Points

  • DOM = Tree structure

  • Har cheez = Node

  • .children ≠ .childNodes

  • Traversal = Parent ↔ Child ↔ Sibling

  • createElement() → memory

  • appendChild() → UI pe show

🔹 1. What is DOM?

  • DOM = Document Object Model
  • It represents your HTML as a tree structure
  • JavaScript can:
    • Create elements
    • Edit elements
    • Delete elements

🔹 2. Selecting Elements

document.querySelector('.class')
document.querySelector('#id')

👉 Advanced selectors:

li:nth-child(2)   // second item
li:first-child    // first item
li:last-child     // last item

🔹 3. Creating Elements

const li = document.createElement('li')

👉 This only creates element (not visible yet)

🔹 4. Adding Content

❌ Not Recommended

li.innerHTML = "Python"

✅ Better

li.textContent = "Python"

✅ Best (Optimized)

li.appendChild(document.createTextNode("Python"))

🔹 5. Adding to DOM

document.querySelector('.language').appendChild(li)

👉 Now element becomes visible on page

🔹 6. Editing Elements

Method 1 (quick but not best)

element.innerHTML = "New Value"

Method 2 (better)

element.textContent = "New Value"

Method 3 (best for replace)

element.replaceWith(newElement)

🔹 7. Removing Elements

element.remove()

👉 Simple:

  1. Select element
  2. Remove it

🔹 8. Key Differences

PropertyMeaning
innerHTMLincludes HTML
textContentonly text
createTextNodeoptimized text insert

🔹 9. Performance Tip ⚡

  • Avoid innerHTML in big apps
  • Prefer:
    • textContent
    • createTextNode
    • appendChild

🔹 10. Golden Rule 🧠

👉 Always:

  • Select → Modify → Append/Remove

🧠 JAVASCRIPT EVENTS + ASYNC — LAST MINUTE NOTES

🟢 1. JavaScript Basics

  • JS is single-threaded
  • JS is synchronous by default
  • Executes line-by-line 
console.log(1);
console.log(2);

🟢 2. Synchronous vs Asynchronous

🔹 Synchronous

  • Line-by-line execution
  • Next waits for previous

🔹 Asynchronous

  • Wait nahi karta
  • Background me task chala jata hai

🟢 3. Blocking vs Non-blocking

  • Blocking → code rukta hai
  • Non-blocking → code continue karta hai

⚠️ Sync ≠ always blocking
⚠️ Async ≠ always non-blocking

🟢 4. JavaScript Runtime System

👉 JS engine akela nahi hota

🔥 Components:

  • Call Stack
  • Web APIs
  • Task Queue
  • Event Loop

🟢 5. Call Stack

  • Functions execute karta hai
  • Follows LIFO (Last In First Out)

🟢 6. Web APIs (Browser)

  • setTimeout
  • setInterval
  • DOM events
  • fetch

👉 Async ka kaam yahin hota hai

🟢 7. Task Queue

  • Queue structure → FIFO
  • Async callbacks wait karte hain

🟢 8. Event Loop (Most Important 🔥)

👉 Kaam:

  • Check karta hai → Call Stack empty hai?
  • Agar empty → queue se function uthata hai

👉 Flow:
Stack → API → Queue → Loop → Stack

🟢 9. setTimeout

setTimeout(fn, time)
  • Ek baar run hota hai
  • Web API me jata hai

⚠️ setTimeout(fn(), 1000) ❌
⚠️ setTimeout(fn, 1000) ✅

🟢 10. setInterval

setInterval(fn, time)
  • Baar-baar run hota hai
  • Stop karne ke liye: 
clearInterval(id)

⚠️ Always store ID

🟢 11. Important Output Trick

console.log(1);

setTimeout(() => console.log(2), 0);

console.log(3);

👉 Output:

1
3
2

🟢 12. Events in JavaScript

👉 User actions:

  • click
  • mousemove
  • keypress

🟢 13. Ways to Add Events

❌ Inline

onclick="..."

⚠️ onclick

element.onclick = fn

✅ Best:

element.addEventListener("click", fn)

🟢 14. Event Object 🔥

element.addEventListener("click", (e) => {})

Important properties:

  • e.target → kis pe click hua
  • e.currentTarget → listener kahan laga hai
  • e.type
  • e.clientX / clientY
  • e.altKey / ctrlKey

🟢 15. Event Propagation

🔹 Bubbling (default)

👉 Child → Parent
IMG → LI → UL

🔹 Capturing

👉 Parent → Child

addEventListener("click", fn, true)

🟢 16. stopPropagation()

👉 Event ko upar jaane se rokta hai

e.stopPropagation()

🟢 17. preventDefault()

👉 Default browser behavior rokta hai

e.preventDefault()

Example:

  • Link redirect stop
  • Form submit stop

🟢 18. Event Delegation 🔥

👉 Parent pe listener lagao

document.querySelector("#images")
.addEventListener("click", (e) => {

  if(e.target.tagName === "IMG"){
    e.target.parentNode.remove();
  }

});

✔ Efficient
✔ Dynamic elements handle

🟢 19. Common Bugs ⚠️

  • ❌ tagName check nahi kiya
  • ❌ multiple intervals start
  • ❌ preventdefault() typo
  • ❌ setTimeout(fn(), time)
  • ❌ queue ko LIFO samajhna

🟢 20. Golden Rules 🔥

  • Always use addEventListener
  • Always store interval/timeout ID
  • Always use checks (if conditions)
  • Understand event flow
  • Async = illusion of parallelism

🧠 SUPER SHORT REVISION (30 sec)

👉 JS = single-threaded
👉 Async = Web API + Event Loop
👉 Queue = FIFO
👉 setTimeout ≠ immediate
👉 Bubbling = child → parent
👉 Capturing = parent → child
👉 stopPropagation → stop bubble
👉 preventDefault → stop default
👉 Event Delegation = parent control

🚀 API + XHR + JSON + this — ONE SHOT REVISION

🌐 1. API – CORE IDEA (MOST IMPORTANT)

👉 API = communication medium between systems

🔁 Flow:

  • Frontend → request bhejta hai
  • Backend → response deta hai

👉 Exchange hota hai:

  • ✅ Data (JSON)
  • ✅ Status (200, 404, etc.)
  • ❌ Variables share nahi hote

📦 2. JSON vs JavaScript Object (CONFUSION BREAKER 💣)

JSONJS Object
String hota haiReal object hota hai
API se aata haiJS me use hota hai
JSON.parse() chahiyeDirect access

🔥 Golden Rule:

typeof xhr.responseText === "string"

👉 Isliye:

const data = JSON.parse(xhr.responseText)

🧠 3. DATA ACCESS (VERY IMPORTANT)

❌ Unsafe:

user.profile.stats.followers

✅ Safe:

user?.profile?.stats?.followers

👉 Agar property missing → crash nahi hoga

😵 4. COMPLEX JSON HANDLE KARNA

🔥 Real Dev Approach:

  1. JSON formatter me paste karo
  2. Root identify karo (object / array)
  3. Important keys dhundo
  4. Step-by-step drill karo
  5. Console me test karo

👉 Shortcut nahi hai ❌
👉 Practice = only way ✔️

⚙️ 5. XMLHttpRequest (OLD BUT IMPORTANT)

🔹 Create:

const xhr = new XMLHttpRequest()

🔹 Setup:

xhr.open('GET', url)

👉 ❗ open = sirf ready karta hai

🔹 Send:

xhr.send()

👉 ❗ send = actual request bhejta hai

🔁 6. readyState (EXAM FAVORITE)

StateMeaning
0Start nahi
1open() call
2headers aaye
3loading
4done

✅ Correct Check:

if (xhr.readyState === 4 && xhr.status === 200)

👉 ❗ Sirf readyState check karna incomplete hai

🔥 7. IMPORTANT EDGE CASE

👉 Agar send() nahi call kiya:

  • readyState → 1 tak jayega
  • request kabhi send nahi hogi

📡 8. EVENT DRIVEN SYSTEM

👉 Meaning:
“jab event ho → tab function chale”

Examples:

  • click → function run
  • readyState change → function run

👉 Ye top-to-bottom execution nahi hai

🔄 9. CALLBACK STYLE

👉 “jab kaam complete ho → tab function run karo”

Example:

xhr.onreadystatechange = function () {}

📥 10. RESPONSE HANDLE KARNA

❌ Direct:

data.followers // undefined

✅ Correct:

const data = JSON.parse(xhr.responseText)
console.log(data.followers)

💣 11. JSON.parse RULE (VERY IMPORTANT)

👉 Sirf string pe use hota hai

❌ Wrong:

JSON.parse(object)

✅ Correct:

JSON.parse(string)

🧠 12. “this” KEYWORD (MOST WEAK AREA ⚠️)

✅ Correct:

xhr.onreadystatechange = function () {
  const data = JSON.parse(this.responseText)
}

👉 yaha:

this === xhr

❌ Dangerous:

xhr.onreadystatechange = () => {
  console.log(this.responseText) ❌
}

👉 Arrow function:

  • apna this nahi hota
  • outer scope ka leta hai

🔥 13. FULL FLOW (INTERVIEW GOLD)

const xhr = new XMLHttpRequest()

xhr.open('GET', url)

xhr.onreadystatechange = function () {
  if (xhr.readyState === 4 && xhr.status === 200) {
    const data = JSON.parse(this.responseText)
    console.log(data)
  }
}

xhr.send()

🧩 14. Web APIs vs Core JS (CONFUSION CLEAR)

👉 Ye sab core JS nahi hain:

  • console
  • document
  • XMLHttpRequest

👉 Ye provide karta hai:

  • Browser / Runtime

⚙️ 15. BIG PICTURE 🧠

👉 3 layers:

  1. Core JS
    • variables, loops, functions
  2. JS Engine (V8)
    • code run karta hai
  3. Runtime (Browser / Node)
    • APIs deta hai (console, DOM, XHR)

🎯 FINAL RAPID FIRE (LAST MIN CHECK)

✔ API → data + request/response
✔ JSON → string
✔ JSON.parse → string → object
✔ open ≠ send
✔ readyState 4 ≠ success
✔ status check zaroori
✔ this (normal fn) = xhr
✔ arrow fn = ❌ dangerous
✔ event-driven = trigger based

⚠️ TUMHARI WEAK POINTS (FOCUS KARNA 🔥)

👉 1. JSON vs Object clarity
👉 2. this vs arrow function (MOST IMPORTANT)
👉 3. safe data access (?.)
👉 4. readyState + status combo

🚀 Pro Tip (Real Dev Mindset)

👉 Jab bhi API use karo:

  1. response type check karo
  2. parse karo
  3. structure samjho
  4. safe access karo

📘 JavaScript Revision Notes (Structured)

🔹 PART 1 – JavaScript Runtime, Engine & Console

🧠 1. Core Idea

JavaScript has 3 layers:

  1. Core JavaScript (ECMAScript)
  2. JavaScript Engine (e.g., V8)
  3. Runtime Environment (Browser / Node.js)

🔹 2. Core JavaScript (ECMAScript)

This is the official language specification.

Includes:

  • Variables
  • Functions
  • Loops
  • Conditions
  • Objects

👉 Important:

  • ❌ No console
  • ❌ No document
  • ❌ No fetch

🔹 3. JavaScript Engine (V8)

👉 Example:

  • Chrome → V8
  • Node.js → V8

What engine does:

  • Converts JS → Machine Code
  • Executes code

👉 Written in:

  • C++

🔁 Execution Flow

Your JS Code → JS Engine (V8) → Machine Code → Output

🔹 4. Runtime Environment

👉 Provides extra features:

  • console
  • DOM
  • fetch
  • setTimeout

Types:

  • Browser
  • Node.js

🔥 5. console.log Reality (Very Important)

👉 console.log() is:

  • ❌ NOT part of JavaScript (ECMAScript)
  • ✅ Provided by Runtime

In Browser:

  • Comes from DevTools (Web API)

In Node.js:

  • Comes from Node runtime

⚡ Key Insight

👉 JavaScript itself cannot print anything

👉 Printing = Runtime responsibility

🔹 6. Console Internals

When you write:

console.log("Hello")

👉 Behind the scenes:

  1. Runtime function is called
  2. Data is processed
  3. Output stream is written
  4. DevTools renders it

🔹 7. Console Methods

  • console.log()
  • console.error()
  • console.warn()
  • console.info()

👉 All are internally different functions

🔹 8. Web APIs (Important)

These are NOT JavaScript features:

  • console
  • document
  • window
  • fetch
  • setTimeout

👉 Provided by:

  • Browser / Runtime

🎯 Final Understanding (Part 1)

ConceptRole
Core JSLogic
Engine (V8)Executes code
RuntimeProvides tools

📘 PART 2 – Promises (Core + Deep Understanding)

🔹 1. What is a Promise?

👉 Definition:
A Promise represents eventual completion or failure of an async operation

🔹 2. Simple Meaning

👉 Promise = Future result

Example:

“I’ll send you the file later”

🔹 3. Promise is an Object

new Promise()

👉 Like:

new Array()

🔹 4. Promise States (VERY IMPORTANT)

StateMeaning
PendingStill running
FulfilledSuccess
RejectedFailed

🔹 5. Internal Properties

  • [[PromiseState]]
  • [[PromiseResult]]

🔹 6. Key Insight

👉 Promise itself is:

  • ✅ Synchronous object

👉 But:

  • Inside task → async

🔹 7. Creating a Promise

const promise = new Promise(function(resolve, reject) {

})

👉 Parameters:

  • resolve → success
  • reject → failure

🔹 8. Important Rule

👉 The function inside Promise:

  • Runs immediately

🔹 9. Example (Async Simulation)

const promiseOne = new Promise(function(resolve, reject) {
    setTimeout(function() {
        console.log("Task done")
        resolve()
    }, 1000)
})

🔹 10. Consuming Promise

promiseOne.then(function() {
    console.log("Promise consumed")
})

🔁 Flow

Promise created
→ Async task runs
→ resolve() called
→ .then() executes

🔹 11. Passing Data

resolve({ name: "John" })
promise.then(function(data) {
    console.log(data)
})

👉 Rule:

resolve(data) → then(data)

🔹 12. Error Handling

reject("Error occurred")
promise
  .then(...)
  .catch(function(error) {
      console.log(error)
  })

🔹 13. Rule

FunctionTrigger
resolve().then()
reject().catch()

🔹 14. Promise Chaining 🔥

promise
  .then(user => {
      return user.name
  })
  .then(name => {
      console.log(name)
  })

👉 Rule:

  • Return value → next .then()

🔹 15. finally()

promise.finally(() => {
    console.log("Done")
})

👉 Runs ALWAYS:

  • success ✅
  • failure ❌

🎯 Final Understanding (Part 2)

  • Promise = object
  • Handles async work
  • Clean alternative to callbacks
  • Supports chaining

✅ Next Parts (coming after you say “proceed”):

  • PART 3 → async/await + Fetch API (deep + traps)
  • PART 4 → Event Loop + Microtask Queue (🔥 most important)
  • PART 5 → OOP + Prototypes (real JS core)
  • PART 6 → Classes + Behind the scenes

📘 PART 3 – async/await + Fetch API (Deep Understanding)

🔹 1. async / await (Modern Promise Handling)

✅ Why introduced?

To make async code:

  • More readable
  • Look like synchronous code

🔹 2. Basic Syntax

async function example() {
    const result = await somePromise
}

🔹 3. Keywords

✅ async

  • Makes a function return a Promise
  • Allows use of await

✅ await

  • Waits for Promise to resolve
  • Pauses execution (non-blocking)

🔹 4. Example

const promise = new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
    setTimeout(() => {
        resolve("Data received")
    }, 1000)
})

async function consume() {
    const response = await promise
    console.log(response)
}

consume()

🔥 Important Insight

👉 await:

  • Stops execution of that function
  • NOT the whole program

🔹 5. Error Problem ⚠️

async function test() {
    const data = await promise
}

👉 If Promise rejects:

  • ❌ Crash

🔹 6. Solution → try...catch

async function test() {
    try {
        const data = await promise
        console.log(data)
    } catch (error) {
        console.log(error)
    }
}

🔁 Flow

await promise → resolve → try block  
await promise → reject → catch block

🔹 7. async/await vs then/catch

Featurethen/catchasync/await
SyntaxChainingClean
ReadabilityMediumHigh
Error Handling.catch()try/catch

🌐 2. Fetch API

🔹 1. What is fetch?

👉 fetch():

  • Sends network request
  • Returns a Promise

🔹 2. Basic Syntax

fetch(url)

🔹 3. Using async/await

async function getData() {
    try {
        const response = await fetch("https://api.example.com/data")
        const data = await response.json()
        console.log(data)
    } catch (error) {
        console.log(error)
    }
}

🔥 MOST IMPORTANT CONCEPT

👉 This line:

const data = await response.json()

👉 Why await again?

Because:

  • response.json() ALSO returns a Promise

❌ Common Mistake

const data = response.json()
console.log(data)

👉 Output:

  • Promise object (NOT actual data)

✅ Correct Flow

fetch() → Promise  
↓  
response  
↓  
response.json() → Promise  
↓  
actual data

🔹 4. Using .then()

fetch(url)
  .then(response => response.json())
  .then(data => console.log(data))
  .catch(error => console.log(error))

🔥 3. Fetch Important Interview Concept

⚠️ Very tricky:

Case 1: Server returns 404

👉 Result:

  • Goes to .then()
  • ❌ NOT an error

Case 2: Network fails (no internet)

👉 Result:

  • Goes to .catch()

🎯 Rule

SituationWhere goes
HTTP error (404, 500).then()
Network failure.catch()

🔹 4. Fetch vs Old Method (XHR)

❌ Old: XMLHttpRequest

Problems:

  • Complex syntax
  • Callback hell
  • Manual JSON parsing

✅ Fetch Advantages

  • Clean syntax
  • Promise-based
  • Works with async/await
  • Easier error handling

🔹 5. Advanced Fetch Options

fetch(url, {
    method: "POST",
    headers: {
        "Content-Type": "application/json"
    },
    body: JSON.stringify(data)
})

🎯 Final Understanding (Part 3)

  • async/await = clean Promise handling
  • fetch = modern network API
  • response.json() = async ⚠️
  • HTTP errors ≠ Promise rejection

📘 PART 4 – Event Loop + Call Stack + Microtask Queue 🔥

🧠 1. JavaScript Engine Structure

Two main parts:

📦 Memory Heap

  • Stores variables

📞 Call Stack

  • Executes functions (LIFO)

🔹 2. Execution Flow

Global Execution Context → pushed first  
Functions → pushed & executed  
→ popped after execution

⚠️ 3. Problem

JavaScript is:
👉 Single-threaded

So how async works?

🌐 4. Web APIs

Examples:

  • setTimeout
  • fetch
  • DOM events

👉 These run outside JS engine

🔹 5. Task Queue (Callback Queue)

Used by:

  • setTimeout
  • setInterval

🔥 6. Microtask Queue (VERY IMPORTANT)

Used by:

  • Promises (.then, .catch)
  • fetch

🆚 7. Priority

QueuePriority
Microtask Queue🔥 High
Task QueueLow

🔁 8. Event Loop

👉 Continuously checks:

Is Call Stack empty?
→ Yes → Take from queue

⚡ 9. Execution Order Rule

👉 Always:

1. Call Stack  
2. Microtask Queue  
3. Task Queue

🔹 10. Example

setTimeout(() => console.log("Timeout"), 0)

Promise.resolve().then(() => console.log("Promise"))

✅ Output:

Promise
Timeout

🔥 Why?

  • Promise → Microtask Queue
  • setTimeout → Task Queue

👉 Microtask runs first

🌐 11. Fetch Special Case

👉 fetch:

  • Returns Promise
  • Uses Microtask Queue

👉 That’s why:

  • It executes before setTimeout

🔹 12. Fetch Internal Working (Deep)

🟡 Step 1: Memory

  • Promise object created
  • Handlers stored internally

🟢 Step 2: Network

  • Request sent via browser/node APIs

🔹 13. Response Handling

Case 1: Success

  • Goes to onFulfilled
  • Promise resolved

Case 2: Failure

  • Goes to onRejected
  • Promise rejected

⚠️ Important Again

👉 404 response:

  • ✅ Request completed
  • ✅ Goes to resolve

🔹 14. Full Flow

fetch()
→ Promise created
→ Network request sent
→ Response received
→ Microtask queue
→ .then() runs

🔥 15. Why Fetch Feels Fast?

👉 Because:

  • Microtask queue = high priority

🎯 Final Understanding (Part 4)

  • JS = single-threaded
  • Async handled via:
    • Web APIs
    • Event Loop
  • Microtask Queue = 🔥 MOST IMPORTANT
